speaker: interviewee
text: Wanda is from the-- left from the Christian name. I was Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ida.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida. Ida Spiegler. And married-- married Ida Mehr. But Wanda is left stuck to me, because nobody remembers me. And no one remembers me as Ida. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So which name would you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Wanda, because if anybody calls me Ida, I look around who it is. It's left Wanda. But that's another story, a very nice story. I have to tell you this about my name, Mehr. Because after the war, it still was Grabowienska.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked for people who knew me. I haven't found anyone from before the war. And no one remembered my name. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come to that later, if we may. Can we talk about the apartment where your family lived? How many rooms in your apartment where your family lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my family, the closest--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parents, sisters, and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were seven of you all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me, six-- six siblings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight, eight all--

speaker: interviewee
text: And parents is eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight. How many rooms were there in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, three, and a kitchen and a hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people slept to a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In each room? [PAUSES] My parents-- and it was quite a large bedroom, the parents', different, of course, than here, because it was a wooden floor, no carpeting. We had rugs on it. And it was considered a nice home there. And my parents and two children in one, the youngest. There was, I remember, a cot for my youngest little brother [PAUSES] and for my youngest sister. And we were in other rooms. In each room was about two, three beds. We have them here, too, up high like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bunks. Was that average for that time? Were there other people living more crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were more crowded. And some had even better conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you didn't have electricity.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't. Later, yes, but for when I was very little, I remember no electricity. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else have electricity in Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no electricity in Grodek. The lamps were on the streets gas, the streets. There was no electricity at all. Lwow had it. And later-- don't remember-- I was still in school when electricity came to Grodek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did people use for lighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Naphtha ?] lamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside, naphthalene or kerosene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, kerosene. I mean, they were wooden stoves where they used to cook and bake. We used a lot of wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: For heating as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, or coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: For heating as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for heating as well. And it was quite warm. It wasn't cold. It's sometimes colder here than it was there, because it was going on all the time in the-- how do you call them? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the oven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like ovens. They were tall ones, where you baked it inside. And it was one where you cooked. And there were kerosene lamps, [? naphtha, ?] inside, two maybe on each side or on the wall, some had. And people were quite happy, because they didn't know anything better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have running water inside the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no running water. No one had it. I remember there was, not far from us, over the road, there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but it was covered around. And we-- we had to pay for a special person who had them with two brackets. There were people, poor people, coming from the country, because it was first floor. And they used to bring us water daily. We had to pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, we had a big-- outside the flat, you opened the door.   On the side was a big barrel, like a barrel, a huge one, filled with water. And that was enough for a day or two. Then he came back. They wanted to earn a-- a few zlotys, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we paid him. And that way, he got to do it, because they earned the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do for a bathroom? What did you have that was used as a bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bathroom? Uh, the same thing. There were no bathrooms attached to the-- or in-- in the flats. But we had a, like a bath, something. You had to fill it with water-- warm it up and fill it with water. And that's how people-- mostly the children. Because the women and the men, or children maybe, in baths [INAUDIBLE]. And the father used to go-- mother, too, although she didn't like it, but she had to go to a mikvah. There was not far from the synagogue a mikvah. And for the men a separate one. So they cleaned themselves at the mikvah. And that had to be done, not only from religious grounds, but to cleanse themselves. And they went to the mikvah, because they were too big for it and it was too much water to bring in water, because we didn't have running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often would they go to the mikvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the mikvah? Eh, they could go any time. Compulsory was once a month, you know, the mikvah was for the women, especially after menstruating. And the men had to go every Friday. But if they want to go more, they paid, and you went-- more than once a week, and go twice a week, or they could go thrice a week. They paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did your father go for a bath?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he went about two or three times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few times weekly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that next to the synagogue or somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was near the synagogue, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Specially for Jewish men, or for everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, only for-- that was a bath not like they have here, somewhere they have baths here-- the community baths or something. No. That was special Jewish, only Jews. That was something like for the women mikvah, that was a bath for the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the-- how was the water heated up at home for the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. At home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, how was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we ba-- on the stove. Barrels of water on the stove. And you poured it in. But there at this mikvah, I don't know how they warmed it. I think through something they had under the floor. I don't know what it is. Never went. I remember when I got married and my mother knew that I will not go to a mikvah. So she went there, paid a few zlotys to the woman and got out a receipt that I was at the mikvah. Never went. And my mother didn't send me [INAUDIBLE]. She didn't. Others, they wouldn't get married without it. But my mother knew straight away that I will not go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a train station in Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Train station? What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Was there a train that went to Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was only one train. As a matter of fact, it's curious. My auntie, my mother's eldest sister-- I think there were about six children. My mother was the youngest. That's what she told me. She said that my auntie-- and my mother was born 1889. It means very near to 900. But my elder sister much, much earlier-- there was I don't know how many years older than my mother. She remembered the first train in Grodek, when that first rain-- train stopped and people ran. They thought that something fell from heaven. They couldn't understand what it is that it came in. And it went. It ran [LAUGHS] without anything, without horses. So she remembered the first train, how everybody ran to the station to look. You know, it was a marvel what happened there. She remembered the first train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of a train was it at Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it steam, a steam train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be, possible. I think the same as now. It's not much changed. Maybe the looks, or the-- you know, but it's very much the same. I was never interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go on a train from Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: A train? I used to go very often to Lwow. I mean, when I finished high school, I went to Lwow because the nearest high school was in Lwow, not in Grodek. And since then, I lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, when I got-- I got married there. Also, my husband came from Grodek. But I lived-- I got married there, because his sister lived in Lwow. His elder sister lived in Lwow. And I stayed with her. And we got married there. My mother came over. So everybody came by train. How else? What other-- what other transport was there in Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carriage and horses, mostly-- horses and carriage. And how do you call [NON-ENGLISH]? You know, it's very old-time English you see in films, those elegant carriages with horses, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: The closed-- the ones that are closed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not always closed. It's open sometimes. And it was very elegant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just return--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were not many cars. As a matter of fact, no one owned a car in Grodek. In Lwow, you could see, but not many on the streets. Since then, how it developed. But if a car came into Grodek, people went out of the streets to look at that marvel, what it is. It goes by itself without horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just returning to your home life again, do you have any-- are your memories of childhood at all-- what I'm trying to ask is, did your family have enough to eat? What was the quality of the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, you mean? Yes, we had enough of that, yes. The children had fruit. I was-- I mentioned before-- as a child, I was vegetarian. I didn't eat meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an interesting point, because did people usually have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my uncle was a shochet, and I have seen the killings of chickens only. And it stopped me from eating meat. Because whenever I've seen soup or-- or a piece of chicken on the table, even Friday nights that we-- it was served-- chicken soup, you know, the tradition, chicken soup. And I ate fish. But I had to convince myself that it's not killed the same way as the chicken. And then I learned at school that fish have cold blood. Fish are cold creatures. They don't have hot blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought, this I can eat, but not meat. Since then, childhood, I never tasted. I don't know even the taste of meat. How often--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm still vegetarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often during the week would your family have meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meat? Oh, about two, three times. But we had also-- they discovered now that fish is very healthy. We had fish then. My mother used to tell him-- tell them that fish is healthy. And my mother knew that fish contains-- contains a lot of iron because of the sea. Oh, she told us some people didn't eat enough, other people, not vegetables. We had a lot of vegetables, because my mother used to say it's very healthy. And something else very curious, tomatoes. You'll have a good laugh. Near us was a-- lived a butcher. But he had it at home, non-Jew, Poles, butchers. Their courtyard was near to ours, to our. Just over, you could look over the windows and see there what goes on in their yard. They used to kill pigs in their yard. And we have seen how they do it. I couldn't look, and I ran away, always. I never went to that window, never. But you could hear the screams. The pigs are screaming, you know. And they used to kill them. Then pieces of-- [PAUSES] meat, szynka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ham, yes. Pieces of ham. Because they sold it from their home. And that yard, where they killed, it was at the back. It was quite a big house, near where we lived. In front, they used to display ham in their windows with-- decorated with tomatoes. We, as children, we always connected it that ham goes together with tomatoes. We never ate tomatoes, because it was decorated around ham with tomatoes. One day, my mother went to the market. And I mentioned before, it was a little market in the center of the city, and brought home tomatoes. We looked at it and said, Mom, that's ham. That's not kosher. Said, no, child. It's very healthy. You can eat tomatoes. Because they put it near ham, that doesn't mean that it's-- it has nothing to do with ham. It is a vegetable or a fruit. You eat. Since then, we ate tomatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other Jewish people at that time, did they also have meat on a regular basis? Was it a habit to eat meat, or only the more--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know then that meat is not so healthy. We know it now, that [PAUSES] other beverages or other vegetables or beans, peas-- [PAUSES] that anything that grows, comes out of the earth, is healthier than meat. Then, people didn't know it. Then it was that a good Jewish home has to have a lot of meat. And quite a number of people died of heart attacks because of too much meat, too much fat around the heart. They didn't know it then. And quite a number-- they used to say a neighbor of ours just got up from bed and fell and died. And they said, we don't know why. He just-- he was healthy. He got up, and he died. My mother explained. He was very a fat, big man. He ate about three times daily meat. And she said, that's the reason. My mother knew then. She read a lot-- medical books a lot. She said, that's the reason, so I won't give my family much-- too much meat. No, not fat, never. No. We ate quite-- at home, we had-- the children had everything. But my mother used to say, don't, please, don't eat that too much. It's not good, and I won't give it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we ate healthy, nearly the same as here now-- very healthy food. And we had adequate food, what we needed. None of us was overweight, not one child, not my mother. My mother and father were rather slim. Were there any big differences in the eating cultures, between the Ukrainians and the Poles and the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the biggest difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, different holidays and different-- as I mentioned before. But it was very hard to live, very scary, when it came, as I said before, to Christmastime [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The-- [PAUSES] it was like in dark ages. We were afraid to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what used to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they used to beat up Jews, especially Ukrainians. Not so Poles, but Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they under the influence of alcohol, or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all, only because we killed Jesus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what they used to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said it's more religious grounds. They used to say it, yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything about--

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to beat up. I remember when I was-- I had a girlfriend. And she just couldn't learn at school. And it was at high school. And I was then about, say, 15 or 16. And her mother used to beg me, come to our place. And I had to run quite-- to say from here to [PAUSES] [INAUDIBLE] Road, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: About three kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they lived, from my place, and no other means of going there. There were no trains, or trams, or trams, or anything, or taxis. So I had to run. And I was little. And when that was snow high up and I had to run, and my mother said, don't go there. I said, but I promised her. She won't have tomorrow done her work to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do you have to help her? Why do you have to go [? run ?] there? I said, I promised free. No one paid me for this anything. But I wanted to help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was not a good student. Not only good, she was a very bad one. She didn't remember anything she learned in school and came home. And I had to write later. I was punished at school for this, because her school work was the same as mine. And I ran all the way to her to do her school work. And then I had to run back. And it was the day before [NON-ENGLISH]. And they used to beat up people in the street, Jews, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Christmas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then finally, I came home. And my mother said, look. We wanted to go out to look for you or to bring you back. They knew where the place is, where I went to. But [PAUSES] maybe they wouldn't do anything to you for a child when you are running. But if my father goes out or mother, they would beat them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home. And they never said even thank you for that, your mother that I did it for her. And at school, when I came back at school, they had to punish me for this. Why did you write her? Let her write-- do it herself. If she doesn't know it, she won't know. So she will not have her school work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Said, but I promised her that I will do it for her. And that was how it was on Christmas mostly, pre-Christmas, Christmas time, on all Catholic holidays. Jews were afraid to go out in the evenings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just Ukrainians or also Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I didn't encounter any Poles, no. Ukrainians. Mostly Ukrainians. But where we lived, there were a lot of Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there also stories about matzah, how matzah was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that label. I know what you mean. No, they didn't mention that. They just said, you killed our Christ, Jesus Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the relations between the Ukrainians and the Poles in Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother-- my little brother Wolf was named after him, after my uncle, my mother's brother. He was married, was older than my mother with two married sons in Lwow [? where he ?] lived. He dealt in-- how do you say [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: In currency? Currency or money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money, yes. I don't know how they call it here. I forgot it. I will recall it. And he used to smuggle from Grodek to Lwow. People gave him money, or he paid for it. Or he bought it up. I don't know. But he smuggled-- smuggled to a bank in Lwow huge amounts of money. He carried that money in a bag. There was special bags around, bags like the doctors carry a bag sometimes, full of money. He didn't expect anything. But the Ukrainians-- there were two brothers and their father. I remember them-- tall, very tall. They were young people and their father, the older father. The father used-- the Ukrainian men. And they lived not far. I remember that [INAUDIBLE]. If I would go there, I would go straight to that place opposite where we lived, everything like yesterday. They lived there. The name was [? Skotnik ?], [NON-ENGLISH]. It's said, you know, every city has a place, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means as they lived. That father spied out-- actually, the two sons. They were grown up. The sons could be about from 20 to 30. They spied on my uncle, my mother's brother, who smuggled the money, carried a lot of money-- I don't know how much exactly-- to Lwow by train. And so he used to go very early in the morning, about, say, 5 o'clock in the morning or even earlier, through that [? Skotnik, ?] through there was a-- a way right to the station, but quite a long way. And he had to pass-- there was a river and a bridge. I remember that, too, that bridge. On that bridge, he had to pass. He went through that bridge, through this, and on this bridge to the station, about, I would say, 100 times. That was his-- not a profession, but that's what he did for living. One morning, that Ukrainians, these boys and their father, they spied on him. They knew what he's doing. They didn't know exactly, but they knew that he's carrying a lot of money. He goes by train to Lwow. They caught him on the bridge. They were two big, tall men. He was-- that uncle was, he's about, say, 60-- between 50 and 60, older than my mother then, though not very strong. They grabbed his bag with the money and threw him into the river. He drowned. So they killed him. [PAUSES] As he didn't come home-- back home, my auntie alarmed the police. He didn't come home. He told-- she told them that he carried money. He had money with him. The police-- you see, also no one likes the Jews there, but not so far. If it's a crime, the police had to find out. The police with dogs [PAUSES] went around. They knew that no Jew would do it. Opposite was that [? Skotnik. ?] They were Ukrainians. They went there. They didn't know where. But the dogs carried them to the house, right to that house. They found out. Then the dogs went with them where did it happen, to the bridge and to the river. And they got him out from the river. He drowned. Of course, they brought him home. [PAUSES] And of course, he was buried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the [HEBREW]. You know what's a [HEBREW]? And my mother went and cried. I was a child then. [PAUSES] Then it went to the court. [PAUSES] They charged those two men with murder and stealing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they got back the money. I don't remember the money back, because they found out everything. Maybe they got back the money. It was still too early. It was [INAUDIBLE] they couldn't dispose of it. But the two men, one said-- said-- my mother told me later-- the one said, it was much earlier, that he didn't know about it. He lied. One was charged with it. And the one was charged-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --for life imprisonment. I don't think they wouldn't then hang you or anything kill him-- life imprisonment. But because he was younger, he said-- no. They charged the father, the father. Sorry. See, that's what I said, that I might mix it up. The father, they did. But the boys knew about it. So they charged the father in life imprisonment. So the young-- the son said, one of them said, we'll go instead of my father. They accepted him. And the father was freed. And the son went for life imprisonment. But these two-- now, see, I jump over. After the war, there were people who survived Grodek. After-- I didn't tell you exactly how they were killed out, all the people in Grodek. They were all killed. No one survived. There was one man whom I knew before the war. He was a brother of my girlfriend, school girlfriend. He survived because-- that has nothing to do with that story-- because a peasant woman who was his nanny at their home took him to her place in a country little place and hid him, two brothers there. She hid them in their place. Still after the war, the younger brother went out and was killed after the war. The way it's known, the [NON-ENGLISH], they called them. [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't know if you know about it. They searched the streets, Poles. And if ever they have seen a survivor, they killed them. They said-- Poles-- they said, Hitler didn't kill enough of you. If one is left, we kill him. They killed him. The other brother, the older one, didn't go out, stayed for quite a while inside [PAUSES] at that place where a Polish woman hid him, hid the two brothers. One died. He went out too early. This one left. I met him-- oh, years, years after the war, when I was already in Australia and went to Israel. I met him in Israel. He told me the story. He told me that he himself, because his brother was killed and all his family in Grodek, he lived later in Lwow. He told me that he-- in Israel-- that he was in Lwow after the war finished. He went on the street in Lwow. Suddenly, he has seen these two brothers. The one was sentenced for life prison, was-- when the Germans came, of course, he was freed. Two brothers, he sees them on the street in [? Munchen, ?] in Germany. He was in [? Munchen. ?] He sees them. As he's seen them, he was a big man, a heavy man. He went up to them, grabbed them both, and started to scream, police, police, police. Police came. He said, these two brothers killed the Jews, helped the Germans to kill the Jews in Grodek. They're from Grodek. With documents, he showed them. That's true. He had them. And he said, these two brothers killed, murdered themselves a Jew-- that means my uncle. And they helped the Nazis to kill, to round up all the Jews in Grodek [INAUDIBLE]. Went to court with them. What money he had, he spent on it. Said, I don't want to be left with a penny. Spent all of them. He said, I have to bring them to court. Brought them to the court in Germany. And they sent-- I don't know where they are. And they sentenced them in Germany for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
